title: Marshmallow root gargle for sore throat
subtitle: A cold-infusion gargle for an inflamed, raw throat
excerpt: Marshmallow root steeped in cold water overnight, used as a gargle for a sore, inflamed throat. The mucilage forms a temporary protective coating on the pharyngeal mucosa, reducing friction and rawness. One of the most effective and overlooked home gargles.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), marshmallow entry, demulcent action on inflamed mucous membranes including the throat. Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), marshmallow for sore throats and catarrh. EMA HMPC monograph on Althaea officinalis radix (2016), traditional use for soothing irritated mucous membranes of the mouth and throat.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale to a full 500 ml jar for a day's supply: 20 g root in 500 ml cold water.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Prepare the night before. The infusion keeps refrigerated for 24 hours.

recipeTools.notes: Any glass jar with a lid for the overnight steep.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preparation that forms a mucilaginous, protective coating on mucous membranes, reducing inflammation and rawness.

content.text: Marshmallow root left to steep in cold water overnight, then used as a gargle for a raw, inflamed throat. The cold-water extraction draws out the root's

mucilage without destroying it, the liquid is slightly viscous and coats the pharyngeal mucosa on contact, reducing the friction and soreness that makes swallowing painful. Maud Grieve lists marshmallow as one of the primary remedies for inflammatory conditions of the throat and mouth; the EMA's 2016 monograph confirms traditional use for soothing irritated mucous membranes. No heat required, cold water is the method.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Medication timing: if also taking marshmallow root internally, take other medications at least one hour apart, the mucilage may slow their absorption.

This is a gargle, not a drink. Swallowing a small amount of the gargle is harmless, but the preparation is dilute enough to drink if preferred.

See your doctor for: a sore throat with a high fever (above 38.5°C), white patches or pus at the back of the throat, difficulty swallowing or breathing, a throat that does not begin to improve after five days, or any throat symptoms in a child under three.

items.prepNote: cut and sifted, about 2 heaped teaspoons
cold, from the tap, not boiled

content.text: Place {{marshmallow-root-dried}} of dried marshmallow root in the glass jar. Pour {{water}} of cold water over. Stir briefly, seal the jar, and leave at room temperature or in the fridge for 6 to 12 hours. Strain through a fine sieve. To use: take a mouthful of the infusion, tilt the head back, and gargle for 20 to 30 seconds, then spit or swallow. Repeat until the cup is finished. The liquid should be slightly viscous and faintly sweet.

Gargle with the full 250 ml preparation three to four times daily, morning, midday, evening, and before bed, for adults. Continue for three to five days or until the throat has settled. If the throat is not improving after five days, see your doctor.

The gargle keeps refrigerated for 24 hours. Use cold or allow to come to room temperature before gargling, a very cold gargle can be uncomfortable on an already sore throat. Make a fresh batch each morning.
When NOT to use this remedy

See your doctor for a sore throat with high fever, white patches or pus on the tonsils (possible streptococcal infection requiring antibiotics), difficulty opening the mouth, severe neck swelling, or any sore throat in a child under three that comes with drooling or difficulty swallowing.
